title: Design a Real-Time Error Log Monitoring System

transcript.text: Design a Real-Time Error Log Monitoring System

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the ingest API and the log schema, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — ingest, alert, query — then go deep on the two or three hardest parts: the 1M/s ingest firehose, sub-second alerting, and making 7 days of logs searchable cheaply. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: this is a **write-dominated firehose** — over a million events a second pouring in — but the two things people actually *want* (an alert in under a second, and a fast debugging search) are *read/compute* problems. So the whole game is decoupling the firehose from the alerting and the querying so one never stalls the other.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**F1 — Ingest logs from many distributed services at >1M/s.**
1. Services should be able to ship error logs to us continuously without blocking their own request path.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Every service in the company — hundreds of them — sends us its error logs as they happen, and sending a log must never slow down the actual product."
3. ⚡ **Why it's hard:** at 1M events/s a single database or a single server is hopeless; we must accept, buffer, and never drop on a burst — back-pressure without data loss is the whole problem.

**F2 — Sub-second alerting on patterns and thresholds.**
1. Users should be able to define rules ("error rate on checkout > 100/min", "any log matching `OutOfMemoryError`") and get notified within a second of the condition being met.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A developer says 'page me if payments start throwing errors,' and we tell them within a second of it actually happening — not five minutes later when it's already an outage."
3. ⚡ **Why it's hard:** you can't run a SQL `COUNT(*)` over a firehose every second; alerting has to be a *streaming* computation over a moving time window, evaluated continuously as data flies past, not a query against stored data.

**F3 — Query/search logs for debugging.**
1. Developers should be able to search recent logs by service, time range, severity, trace ID, and free text, and get results in a couple of seconds.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A developer debugging a 2am incident types 'show me all errors from the payments service in the last 10 minutes containing this order ID' and gets an answer fast enough to keep debugging."
3. ⚡ **Why it's hard:** free-text search across tens of terabytes a day needs an inverted index, and indexing everything at full-text granularity at 1M/s is brutally expensive — we have to be smart about what we index and how long we keep it hot.

**Below the line (out of scope for now):** dashboards/visualization (Grafana-style — assume a thin read layer on top of query API), log *parsing*/structured extraction beyond a basic schema, anomaly-detection ML, multi-tenant billing, and long-term (>30 day) compliance archival beyond a cold S3 dump.

### Non-Functional

**N1 — Ingest availability & durability: 99.99% accept, zero acknowledged-log loss.**
1. The ingest path should accept writes at four-nines availability and must not lose a log once it's acknowledged.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Four-nines means the ingest endpoint is down less than ~52 minutes per *year* total, and once we say 'got it' to a service, that log is safe."
3. 💥 **What breaks without it:** if ingest drops logs silently, the one error that explains the outage is the one we lost — the system is worse than useless because people *trust* it during incidents.

**N2 — Alert latency: p99 under 1 second, end to end.**
1. The system should fire an alert within 1s (p99) of the triggering log being ingested.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 times out of 100, from the moment the bad log arrives to the moment the pager buzzes is under one second."
3. 💥 **What breaks without it:** a 5-minute alert lag turns a blip into a full outage; the entire value proposition ("catch it before customers do") evaporates.

**N3 — Query latency: p99 a few seconds over the hot (7-day) window.**
1. Search over the recent hot window should return in ~2–3s p99.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A debugging search over the last week comes back in a couple of seconds, not a couple of minutes."
3. 💥 **What breaks without it:** if search takes 30s, engineers stop using it mid-incident and go grep raw files — the tool is abandoned exactly when it matters.

**N4 — Scalability & elasticity: linear scale-out to 2M/s peak.**
1. The system should absorb 2x bursts (deploys, incidents cause log storms) by adding nodes, not by falling over.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When something breaks and every service starts screaming at once — that's a 2x log storm — we add capacity instead of crashing."
3. 💥 **What breaks without it:** the worst log storms happen *during* incidents; if the monitoring system collapses under the storm, you're blind exactly when you need eyes most.

**N5 — Cost efficiency.**
1. The system should keep $/GB ingested low — log data is high-volume, low-value-per-byte.
2. 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Logs are cheap individually but there are trillions of them, so the bill is dominated by storage and indexing — we have to be ruthless about tiering."
3. 💥 **What breaks without it:** naively full-text-indexing 88 TB/day in a hot search cluster costs more than the product it's monitoring; the project gets cancelled on cost.

⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** exactly-once alerting is expensive — I'll target *at-least-once with idempotent alert dedup* (an alert may be *evaluated* twice but the user is paged once), and I'll call that out in the ledger rather than pretend we get exactly-once for free.

> 💬 "So the shape is: a huge dumb-fast write path, a streaming compute path hanging off it for alerts, and an indexed read path for search — three consumers of one log stream, each scaled independently."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "At its core this is three jobs sharing one firehose. First, *ingest* — over a million logs a second from hundreds of services, and once we ack a log we can't lose it. Second, *alerting* — developers set rules and we have to page them in under a second, which means a streaming computation over a moving window, not a query. Third, *search* — fast free-text debugging over the last week. The non-functionals that drive everything are: never drop an acked log, sub-second alerts, and keep cost sane because the volume is enormous. The one thing I'll flag now is alerting is at-least-once with dedup, not exactly-once — I'll defend that later."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — What's the average log size, and is there a hard max?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Bandwidth and storage scale with bytes, not just count — 300 bytes versus 2 KB is a 6x difference in everything downstream, so this number sizes the whole system."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If small (~300B, structured) → cheaper, can keep more hot, simpler.
- If large (multi-KB stack traces) → must cap/truncate at ingest, compression and tiering become critical.
**Assumption:** ~1 KB average per log (structured JSON + a trace), hard cap 64 KB with truncation. That gives ~1 GB/s ingest bandwidth.

**Q2 — How fresh must alerts be, really — is 1s the bar or is 5s fine?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Sub-second alerting forces an in-memory stream processor; if 5–10s is acceptable, I can use cheaper micro-batching and save a lot of complexity."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If sub-second → in-memory streaming (Flink/Kafka Streams), windows in RAM.
- If minutes OK → periodic batch over the warehouse, far simpler/cheaper.
**Assumption:** p99 < 1s — streaming it is.

**Q3 — How long do logs need to be searchable, and at what granularity?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Retention times the daily volume *is* the storage bill; 7 days hot versus 90 days hot is a 10x cost swing."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If 7 days hot is enough → small hot index, cheap.
- If 90 days full-text → huge index cost, must rethink tiering.
**Assumption:** 7 days hot/searchable, 30 days in cheap cold storage (S3) queryable with higher latency, then delete.

**Q4 — Is it acceptable to lose a *tiny* fraction of logs under extreme overload, or is zero-loss absolute?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Zero-loss-always means I must buffer durably before acking and never drop; if best-effort is OK under storm, I can shed load and stay up cheaper."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If zero-loss → durable buffer (Kafka) with back-pressure, clients retry.
- If best-effort under extreme load → sample/drop low-severity logs first.
**Assumption:** zero loss for **acked** logs; under extreme overload we apply back-pressure + client-side sampling of low-severity (INFO/DEBUG) logs, never errors.

**Q5 — Do alerts need to be exactly-once (page once, guaranteed), or is at-least-once with dedup fine?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Exactly-once across a distributed stream is very expensive; at-least-once with an idempotency key on the alert is nearly as good and far simpler."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If exactly-once required → transactional sink, slower.
- If at-least-once + dedup → dedup store with TTL on (rule_id, window) keys.
**Assumption:** at-least-once evaluation, **deduped to once-per-user-per-window** via a Redis key.

**Q6 — Roughly how many developers query, and how often? Is query QPS material?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If query load is thousands/sec it competes with ingest for resources; if it's a handful of engineers debugging, query is a rounding error and I optimize purely for ingest."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If high query QPS → dedicated read replicas, query caching.
- If low → single search tier, optimize for write.
**Assumption:** ~5,000 engineers, ~20 queries/day each → **~1–2 queries/s**. Negligible vs ingest; I'll spend my budget on the write path.

**Q7 — Are logs already structured, or raw text we must parse?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If they arrive structured (JSON with service, level, trace_id) I index cheaply; if raw text I need a parsing tier and grok patterns, which adds cost and failure modes."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If structured → index selected fields, store body as blob.
- If raw → add a parse/enrich stage in the stream.
**Assumption:** mostly structured JSON via a shared logging library; we index key fields and keep the raw body.

**Q8 — How many distinct alert rules, and how complex (simple threshold vs joins across services)?**
🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Ten thousand simple thresholds is easy to fan out; cross-service correlation rules need stateful joins and change the stream topology."
↔️ **Design fork:**
- If simple thresholds/patterns → per-rule counters in the stream.
- If correlation/joins → stateful keyed windows, bigger state store.
**Assumption:** thousands of rules, mostly per-service threshold + regex pattern matches; a handful of stateful aggregations. No cross-service joins in v1.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my assumptions — 1 KB logs, sub-second alerts, 7-day hot retention, zero-loss on acked logs, at-least-once deduped alerts. Before I size it: anything there you'd push back on, or should I run with it?"

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll only compute the few numbers that actually drive a decision. Everything's rounded to say-out-loud figures.

**QPS** = queries (here, *logs*) per second — how many events hit us each second. **Ingest bandwidth** = bytes/second flowing in. **Partition** = one ordered shard of the log stream that a single consumer thread reads.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest rate | given | 1,000,000/s | "a million logs a second" | nothing single-node survives this — must shard the buffer |
| Peak ingest | 1M × 2 (storm/deploy burst) | 2,000,000/s | "two million a second at peak" | size for 2x → ~40+ ingest nodes |
| Ingest bandwidth | 1M logs/s × 1 KB = 1,000,000,000 B/s | ~1 GB/s | "about a gigabyte a second, ~8 gigabit" | NIC/partition planning; compress on the wire |
| Logs/day | 1M/s × 86,400 s = 86,400,000,000 | ~86 billion/day | "around 86 billion logs a day" | nothing relational holds this |
| Raw storage/day | 86.4B × 1 KB = 88,473,600,000,000 B | ~88 TB/day raw | "~88 terabytes a day raw" | must compress + tier |
| Compressed/day | 88 TB ÷ 8 (logs compress ~8–10x) | ~11 TB/day | "~11 terabytes a day compressed" | hot-index sizing |
| 7-day hot footprint | 11 TB × 7 | ~77 TB | "~77 terabytes hot" | search cluster disk |
| 30-day cold footprint | 11 TB × 30 | ~330 TB (~0.33 PB) | "about a third of a petabyte for a month" | S3 tiering, not hot disk |
| Kafka partitions | 1 GB/s ÷ ~10 MB/s per partition | ~100 (200 at peak) | "~100–200 partitions" | parallelism unit for ingest + consumers |
| Ingest nodes | 2M/s ÷ ~50K msg/s per node | ~40 nodes | "around 40 stateless ingest nodes" | horizontal, cheap, autoscale |
| Query QPS | 5,000 devs × 20/day ÷ 86,400 | ~1–2/s | "basically nothing" | optimize for writes, not reads |

The ONE number that forces the design: **1M logs/s write vs ~1–2 queries/s read — that's a ~600,000:1 write-to-read ratio.** The flip threshold: if query QPS ever climbs into the thousands/s (e.g. every dashboard auto-refreshes against us), I'd add dedicated read replicas and a query cache. Until then, **every architectural dollar goes to the write/ingest path.**

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that matters is a million logs a second coming in, against maybe one or two searches a second going out — so this is overwhelmingly a write problem, roughly a hundred-thousand to one. That tells me to spend everything on a fast, durable, horizontally-sharded ingest path and a streaming alert path, and treat search as a cheap afterthought sitting on a 7-day index. Storage-wise it's about 11 terabytes a day compressed, so retention and tiering — not the database — are the real cost story."

---

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the entities — I'll add full field definitions in §7 (Data Model).

**LogEvent** — the atomic unit: one error/log line from one service at one instant. Owns the raw message, severity, timestamp, service identity, and trace/correlation IDs.
🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of a LogEvent like a single line in a giant shared diary — who wrote it (service), when, how urgent (severity), and what happened (message)."
⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "They'll ask: 'how do you keep these ordered and not lose any at a million a second?' → answer: we don't order globally; we partition by service and rely on a durable append-only log (Kafka) that buffers before we ack, so a slow consumer never drops data."

**AlertRule** — a user-defined condition over the stream: a pattern (regex/field match) or a threshold (count over a window) plus where to notify.
🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of an AlertRule like a smoke detector you place in a specific room — 'if smoke in the kitchen exceeds X, ring this bell.'"
⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "They'll ask: 'how do you evaluate thousands of rules over a firehose in under a second?' → answer: rules compile into per-key counters/matchers inside a stream processor that updates as each event passes, so evaluation is O(1) per event, not a scan."

**AlertEvent (Notification)** — a fired instance of a rule: which rule, which window, the matching evidence, dedup key, and delivery state.
🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of an AlertEvent like the actual phone call the smoke detector makes — and we make sure it only calls you once per fire, not 500 times."
⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "They'll ask: 'won't a sustained error spike page me every second?' → answer: each fire carries a dedup key of (rule_id, window) written to Redis with a TTL, so we suppress repeats within the cooldown."

**ServiceSource** — metadata about a sender: service name, team owner, environment, expected volume (for anomaly baselines and routing).
🗣️ **Simple analogy:** "Think of ServiceSource like the contact card for each service — who owns it, where it runs, how chatty it normally is."
⚠️ **Interviewer probe:** "They'll ask: 'how do you route an alert to the right team?' → answer: the rule and the ServiceSource together carry the on-call/notification target, resolved at fire time."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four entities. The LogEvent is the firehose unit — keep it tiny and immutable. The AlertRule is a user's standing condition, and the surprising one is the AlertEvent: the hard part isn't firing, it's *not* firing five hundred times during a spike, so it carries a dedup key. ServiceSource is just metadata that tells us who owns what and where to page. I'll flesh out fields and types in the data model section."

---

## 5. API / Interface

REST for ingest and query (simple, cacheable, universal); a high-throughput binary/batch endpoint for the firehose. Security: senders authenticate with a per-service API key/mTLS; the server stamps `received_at` and `service_id` from the authenticated identity — **never trusts a client-supplied service id or timestamp for billing/routing trust** (we keep the client `event_time` too, but mark which is which).

### F1 — Ingest logs

**`POST /v1/logs:batch`** — POST because it creates new state (appends events). Batched because per-event HTTP at 1M/s is impossible; clients buffer ~100–1,000 events and ship one compressed request.
- **Key request fields:**
  - `events: LogEvent[]` — the batch; the whole point.
  - `event.level: enum` — severity; drives sampling/routing (errors never sampled).
  - `event.service: string` — *advisory*; the trusted service id comes from the auth key, this is for sub-component tagging.
  - `event.event_time: timestamp` — client clock; we ALSO stamp server `received_at` because client clocks lie.
  - **Absent on purpose:** no `received_at` from the client and no `partition` — the server assigns both, so a client can't skew our windows or hot-spot a partition.
- **Concrete example:**
```
// Request
POST /v1/logs:batch        (Content-Encoding: gzip, Authorization: ApiKey svc_payments)
{
  "events": [
    { "event_time":"2026-06-19T14:32:00.123Z", "level":"ERROR",
      "service":"payments.charge", "trace_id":"tr_9f2", "msg":"OutOfMemoryError: heap" }
  ]
}

// Response 202
{ "accepted": 1, "rejected": 0, "offset_hint": "p7@10482931" }
```
- **One key design decision:** returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — we've *durably buffered* the batch (written to Kafka), not fully processed/indexed it. We ack only after the durable append, so "accepted" means "safe," and the client can stop retrying. This is the zero-loss contract.

### F2 — Manage & receive alerts

**`POST /v1/rules`** — POST creates a new standing rule.
- **Key request fields:** `condition` (threshold or pattern), `window` (e.g. 60s), `target` (pagerduty/slack/webhook), `cooldown`. The server sets `rule_id`, `owner` (from JWT), `created_at`.
- **Example:**
```
// Request
POST /v1/rules
{ "name":"payments-error-spike",
  "condition": { "type":"threshold", "field":"level", "eq":"ERROR",
                 "service":"payments.*", "count_gt":100 },
  "window":"60s", "cooldown":"300s", "target":"pd:payments-oncall" }

// Response 201
{ "rule_id":"rule_7Ab", "status":"ACTIVE", "owner":"u_123" }
```
- **Design decision:** `owner` is server-set from the session — you can't create rules in someone else's name. Rule changes publish to a control topic the stream processors subscribe to, so updates take effect in seconds without redeploy.

### F3 — Query logs

**`GET /v1/logs:search`** — GET because it's a pure read; cacheable, no state change.
- **Key request fields (query params):** `service`, `level`, `from`/`to` (time range — *required*, bounds the scan), `q` (free text), `trace_id`, `limit`, `cursor` (pagination).
- **Absent on purpose:** no unbounded "search everything" — a time range is mandatory so a query can't scan all 77 TB.
- **Example:**
```
GET /v1/logs:search?service=payments.charge&level=ERROR&from=2026-06-19T14:20:00Z
    &to=2026-06-19T14:35:00Z&q=OutOfMemoryError&limit=50

// Response 200
{ "hits": [ { "event_time":"...","service":"payments.charge","trace_id":"tr_9f2","msg":"..." } ],
  "total": 142, "cursor": "eyJvZmZzZXQiOjUwfQ" }
```
- **Design decision:** mandatory time range + cursor pagination keeps every query bounded; the index is time-partitioned so a 15-minute search touches one or two shards, not the whole cluster.

> 💬 "The key API insight: ingest returns 202 *only after durable buffering* — that single contract is how we promise zero loss without making the client wait for indexing."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Three surfaces. Ingest is a batched, compressed POST that returns 202 the moment we've durably buffered the events — that 'durably buffered' is the zero-loss promise, and it's why we batch and ack fast instead of indexing synchronously. Rules are a simple CRUD where the owner is taken from the session, never the request, and rule changes flow to the processors over a control topic so they're live in seconds. Search is a plain GET with a *mandatory* time range so no one can accidentally scan a week of data, plus cursor pagination."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

I'll build ingest first (the foundation), then alerting and search as two consumers hanging off the same durable log. Starting minimal.

### 6.1 "Ingest logs from many services at >1M/s"

The user (a service) fires logs continuously; the flow is interesting because it must accept 1M/s, never block the sender, and never lose an acked event — all while being cheap and stateless to scale.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Load balancer | A traffic cop that spreads incoming batches across many identical ingest servers | Need to fan 2M/s across ~40 nodes evenly with health checks | Not DNS round-robin (no health awareness, uneven); not a single node (instant death) | One more hop (~sub-ms) |
| Ingest service (stateless collectors) | A thin server that validates a batch, stamps server time, and appends it to the log buffer — holds no state itself | Stateless = scale by adding boxes, any node handles any batch, trivial autoscale | Not writing straight to a DB (no DB takes 1M/s of inserts); not stateful nodes (rebalancing pain) | Does almost no work itself — by design |
| Kafka (durable log buffer) | A durable, append-only, partitioned commit log that holds events on disk and lets multiple consumers read at their own pace | Need a buffer that absorbs bursts, never loses acked data, and feeds 3 independent consumers — exactly Kafka's job | Not a raw queue like SQS (no replay, weak multi-consumer, cost at this volume); not Redis (in-memory, loses data on crash) | Operational complexity of running Kafka |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "In front I put a load balancer just to spread 2 million requests a second across a fleet. Behind it are stateless collector nodes — they do almost nothing on purpose: authenticate, stamp a server timestamp, and append the batch to Kafka. I keep them stateless so I scale purely by adding boxes. The heart is Kafka — think of it as a durable, replayable tape that buffers the firehose on disk and lets my alerting and my search consumers each read at their own speed without stepping on each other. I rejected writing straight to a database because nothing relational survives a million inserts a second, and I rejected a plain queue because I need *replay* and *multiple independent readers*, which Kafka gives me and SQS doesn't."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Load balancer** — a component that distributes incoming requests across a pool of identical servers and skips unhealthy ones. Here it spreads 2M/s across ~40 stateless collectors.
> **Stateless service** — a server that keeps no local data between requests, so any node can handle any request and you scale by cloning it. Here it lets ingest scale linearly and autoscale on bursts.
> **Kafka** — a durable, append-only commit log split into partitions; producers append, multiple consumer groups read independently and can replay from any offset. Here it's the shock absorber that buffers the firehose durably and feeds alerting and search separately.
> **Partition** — one ordered slice of a Kafka topic that exactly one consumer in a group reads at a time. Here partitions are our unit of parallelism — ~100–200 of them.
> **Offset** — a consumer's bookmark into a partition. Here it's how a slow consumer catches up later without losing data.

**DIAGRAM — Ingest path**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Svc[Services - 100s of senders]
    Svc -->|"1: POST /logs:batch gzip"| LB[Load Balancer]
    LB -->|"2: spread batch"| ING[Ingest collectors - stateless x40]
    ING -->|"3: append + ack 202"| K[[Kafka - logs topic ~150 partitions]]
    K -.->|"4: replicated x3"| K
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Svc client
    class LB,ING svc
    class K queue
```

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Services POST a compressed batch.** Each sender buffers ~100–1,000 events and ships one gzip'd request. Why batched: per-event HTTP at 1M/s would be ~1M connections/s — impossible; batching cuts request rate ~1000x. State change: none yet on our side. What goes wrong: if our endpoint is slow, the client buffers and retries with backoff — it never blocks its own request path.
2. **LB spreads the batch** to any healthy collector. Why the LB owns this: collectors are identical and stateless, so even distribution + health-checking is all that's needed. State change: a node is now handling this batch. What goes wrong: a dead node fails its health check and is pulled; the batch is retried elsewhere.
3. **Collector validates, stamps `received_at` and trusted `service_id`, appends to Kafka, returns 202.** Why here: the collector owns the trust boundary (auth → identity) and the durability boundary (ack only after Kafka confirms the append). State change: the events are now durably on disk in Kafka — *this* is the zero-loss moment. What goes wrong: if Kafka is unreachable, the collector returns 5xx and the client retries; we never ack data we didn't persist.
4. **Kafka replicates each partition 3x** across brokers before considering a write committed. State change: the event survives a broker failure. What goes wrong: if a broker dies, a replica is promoted; producers retry the in-flight batch (idempotent producer dedups it).

> 💬 "The whole trick of ingest is: do nothing expensive synchronously — authenticate, stamp, durably append, ack. Everything hard happens *downstream* of Kafka, off the sender's critical path."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Ingest is deliberately boring. Services batch and compress their logs and POST them; a load balancer sprays those across a fleet of stateless collectors. A collector's only job is to authenticate the sender, stamp a trusted server timestamp, and append the batch to Kafka — a durable, partitioned log that buffers everything on disk. The instant Kafka confirms the append, we return 202, and that's our zero-loss promise: we only say 'got it' once it's safely replicated three ways. I keep collectors stateless so I scale to 2 million a second just by adding nodes, and I push all the real work downstream."

### 6.2 "Sub-second alerting on patterns and thresholds"

Now a *consumer* reads the same Kafka stream and continuously evaluates rules. Interesting because we can't query stored data every second — we compute over a moving window in memory as events fly by.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stream processor (Flink) | An engine that runs a continuous computation over a stream, keeping per-key counters and time windows in memory and updating them per event | Need stateful windowed aggregation at 1M/s with sub-second latency + exactly-once *state* — Flink's core competency | Not a cron/batch query (minutes of lag, scans storage); not Kafka Streams only (heavier stateful ops/scale are easier in Flink) | Operational weight of a Flink cluster |
| Rule store (Postgres) | A small relational DB holding rule definitions, owners, targets | Rules are low-volume, relational, need transactions/CRUD | Not in Flink memory only (lost on restart, no CRUD UI) | Extra component, but tiny |
| Dedup store (Redis) | An in-memory key-value store with TTLs, used to suppress duplicate alerts | Need a fast atomic "have we paged for (rule,window) already?" check with auto-expiry | Not Postgres (too slow/hot for per-fire checks); not in-memory map (not shared across Flink tasks) | Eventual: a rare race could double-fire (acceptable) |
| Notifier service | A small service that delivers a fired alert to PagerDuty/Slack/webhook with retries | Decouples "decide to fire" from "deliver," so a slow pager API can't back up the stream | Not delivering from inside Flink (blocking I/O stalls the stream) | One more async hop |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "A Flink job subscribes to the same Kafka topic — Flink is an engine that keeps running counters and time windows in memory and updates them as each event passes, so checking 'did payments errors exceed 100 in the last minute' is just reading a counter, not scanning a database. Rules live in a small Postgres and are pushed to Flink over a control topic so edits go live in seconds. When a rule trips, before paging I do an atomic check-and-set in Redis on the (rule, window) key — that's my dedup, so a sustained spike pages you once, not 500 times. Actual delivery goes to a separate notifier so a slow PagerDuty API can't stall my stream. I rejected running this as a periodic batch query because that's minutes of lag, and the whole requirement is sub-second."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Flink (stream processor)** — an engine that runs a non-stop computation over a data stream, maintaining state (counters, windows) in memory with checkpointing for fault tolerance. Here it evaluates thousands of alert rules per event in real time.
> **Tumbling/sliding window** — a moving time bucket (e.g. "the last 60 seconds") over which you aggregate. Here it's how "100 errors per minute" is computed continuously.
> **Keyed state** — per-key counters Flink holds in memory, partitioned by (service, rule). Here it makes per-event evaluation O(1) instead of a scan.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store with atomic ops and TTLs. Here it dedups alerts: SET-if-absent on (rule_id, window) with a cooldown TTL.
> **Checkpointing** — Flink periodically snapshots its in-memory state to durable storage so it can recover exactly where it left off. Here it means an alert window survives a processor crash.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Flink consumes the logs topic** in parallel across partitions, updating per-(service, rule) counters in keyed windows. Why Flink owns it: it's the only piece that holds windowed state in memory at this rate. State change: in-memory counters advance. What goes wrong: a Flink task dies → it restores from its last checkpoint and replays from the saved Kafka offset, losing no window.
2. **Rule edits flow from Postgres over a control topic** into Flink. Why: lets users change rules without redeploying the job. State change: the active rule set updates live. What goes wrong: a malformed rule is rejected at the API; the stream keeps the last good set.
3. **A rule trips** (count > threshold, or a pattern matches). Flink emits a candidate alert. State change: a fire candidate exists. What goes wrong: clock skew on event_time → we window on `received_at` (server time) to keep windows sane.
4. **Dedup check in Redis:** `SET (rule_id:window) NX EX cooldown`. If it's the first fire in the window, we proceed; otherwise we drop it. Why Redis: atomic, fast, auto-expiring. State change: a dedup key now exists for the cooldown. What goes wrong: Redis down → fail *open* (page anyway) so we never miss a real alert, accepting occasional duplicates.
5. **Notifier delivers** to PagerDuty/Slack/webhook with retries and its own queue. Why separate: blocking I/O must not stall the stream. State change: alert delivered, AlertEvent recorded. What goes wrong: pager API down → retry with backoff + fall back to a secondary channel.

> 💬 "The insight: alerting is a *standing computation over a moving window*, not a query — and the only stateful trick that matters is deduping the fire, not detecting it."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Alerting is a Flink job reading the same Kafka stream. Flink keeps per-rule counters in memory over time windows, so 'more than 100 payment errors in the last minute' is just a counter crossing a line — no database scan, which is how we hit sub-second. Rules live in Postgres and stream into Flink over a control topic so edits are live in seconds. The genuinely hard part isn't detecting the spike, it's not paging you 500 times during it — so before firing, I do an atomic set-if-absent in Redis keyed on the rule and the time window, with the cooldown as the TTL. Delivery is handed to a separate notifier so a slow pager API never backs up the stream, and if Redis is down I fail open and page anyway."

### 6.3 "Query/search logs for debugging"

A second consumer indexes the same stream for search. Interesting because we want fast free-text over 7 days but indexing everything is the dominant cost — so we tier aggressively.

**ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indexer (consumer) | A consumer that reads Kafka, batches events, and writes them into the search store and cold storage | Decouples indexing speed from ingest; can lag without dropping data | Not indexing inside collectors (couples ingest to slow index) | Search is seconds-behind real time (fine) |
| Search store (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch) | A search engine that pre-builds an inverted index (word → which logs) so text queries return in ms | Need ranked free-text + field filters over ~77 TB hot in seconds — no SQL does this | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not grep on files (no index, minutes) | Index storage + cost; eventual freshness |
| Object storage (S3) | Cheap, durable bulk storage for compressed raw logs | Need 30-day cheap archive + rebuild source for the index at ~$/TB | Not keeping 30 days in ES (10x the cost) | Cold queries are slower (scan/restore) |
| Time-based index rollover | New ES index per hour/day; old ones age out to cold/delete | Lets us drop a whole day by deleting one index — cheap retention | Not one giant index (deletes are expensive, no tiering) | Slightly more index management |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "A separate indexer consumes Kafka and writes into Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds an inverted index, basically a map from each word to the logs that contain it, so a text search is a lookup, not a scan. I only keep 7 days hot in ES because indexing is the expensive part; in parallel the indexer dumps the compressed raw logs to S3 for a cheap 30-day archive. I roll indices by time — a fresh index per day — so aging data out is just deleting yesterday's index, which is instant and cheap. I rejected SQL `LIKE` because it scans every row with no ranking, and grepping raw files because there's no index — both are minutes, and we need seconds."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Indexer** — a consumer that transforms stream events into searchable documents and writes them to the search store. Here it's the bridge from Kafka to Elasticsearch and S3.
> **Inverted index** — a precomputed map from each term to the list of documents containing it, which is what makes full-text search fast. Here it powers free-text log search in milliseconds.
> **Elasticsearch / OpenSearch** — a distributed search engine built on inverted indices with field filters and ranking. Here it serves the 7-day hot search.
> **Object storage (S3)** — cheap, durable, infinitely scalable bulk file storage billed per GB. Here it's the 30-day cold archive and the rebuild source for the index.
> **Index rollover** — creating a new time-bounded index periodically so retention = deleting old indices. Here it makes 7-day retention an O(1) delete, not a mass row deletion.

**NUMBERED STEP NARRATION**
1. **Indexer consumes Kafka and batches** events into bulk writes. Why batch: ES bulk indexing is far more efficient than per-doc. State change: events buffered for index. What goes wrong: ES backpressure → indexer slows its consumption (Kafka holds the backlog), search lags but nothing is lost.
2. **Bulk-write into the current hot ES index** (rolled by time). State change: events become searchable within seconds. What goes wrong: an ES node dies → replica shard serves; indexer retries the bulk batch (idempotent by doc id = partition+offset).
3. **Same indexer dumps compressed raw to S3** for the 30-day cold tier. State change: durable cheap archive exists. What goes wrong: S3 write fails → retry; independent of the hot path.
4. **Developer issues a bounded search.** Time range is mandatory. State change: none (read). What goes wrong: too-broad range → API enforces a max span and paginates.
5. **Query service hits ES** for hot-window searches → ms–seconds. State change: none. What goes wrong: hot shard overloaded → query cache + the ~1–2 QPS reality means this is rarely an issue.
6. **For older data, query service reads S3** (slower, scan/restore). State change: none. What goes wrong: cold query is slow → we set expectations (cold = seconds-to-minutes) and offer async export.

> 💬 "The insight: search rides the *same* Kafka stream as a third independent consumer, and we keep only 7 days hot because indexing — not storage — is the cost; everything older is cheap S3."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Search is just a third consumer on the same Kafka stream. An indexer batches events into Elasticsearch, which keeps an inverted index — a precomputed word-to-logs map — so a free-text query over the last week comes back in seconds instead of scanning terabytes. The same indexer also dumps compressed raw logs to S3 for a cheap 30-day archive. The cost trick is keeping only 7 days hot and rolling indices by day, so dropping old data is a one-shot index delete. Queries are forced to carry a time range so nobody can accidentally scan the whole week."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — ENTITY TABLE**

| Entity | Key fields (type; PK/shard key noted) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LogEvent | `event_id` (ULID, PK), `service_id` (str, **shard key**), `level` (enum), `event_time` (ts), `received_at` (ts), `trace_id` (str), `msg` (text), `attrs` (json) | Kafka (transit) → Elasticsearch (hot 7d) + S3 (cold 30d) | `service_id` (Kafka), time (ES indices) | Eventual (search lags secs) |
| AlertRule | `rule_id` (PK), `owner_id`, `condition` (json), `window` (int s), `cooldown` (int s), `target` (str), `status` (enum) | PostgreSQL | `rule_id` | Strong (CRUD, transactional) |
| AlertEvent | `alert_id` (PK), `rule_id` (FK), `window_start` (ts), `evidence` (json), `dedup_key` (str, **unique**), `delivery_state` (enum) | PostgreSQL (record) + Redis (dedup) | `rule_id` | Strong record; eventual dedup |
| ServiceSource | `service_id` (PK), `team`, `env`, `expected_rate`, `notify_default` | PostgreSQL | `service_id` | Strong |

**PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS**

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: LogEvent (in transit, the firehose buffer)**
1. **What it is (plain words):** "Kafka is a durable, append-only log split into partitions — producers append events to the end, and multiple independent readers each keep their own bookmark and read at their own pace; everything's written to disk and replicated."
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** we need to absorb 1M+/s durably, survive bursts, and feed *three* independent consumers (alerting, indexing, archival) from one write — Kafka's multi-consumer replayable log is exactly this; a slow consumer can't lose data, it just lags.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **AWS SQS/RabbitMQ** — a classic queue, but it deletes on consume so it can't fan out to three independent readers with replay, and at this volume it's costly and order-less.
   - **Redis Streams** — in-memory, so a crash risks data and it can't durably hold a multi-TB backlog during a long consumer outage.
   - **Write straight to ES/DB** — nothing accepts 1M inserts/s reliably; the buffer is non-negotiable.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** operational complexity of running and balancing a large Kafka cluster, and partition-count planning.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Kafka's the shock absorber. It's a durable replayable log, so I can take a million events a second, never lose an acked one, and let alerting, search, and archival all read the same stream independently. I looked at a plain queue, but it deletes on read and can't fan out with replay, which is the whole point here."

**🗄️ Elasticsearch — used for: LogEvent (hot 7-day search)**
1. **What it is (plain words):** "Elasticsearch is a search engine that pre-builds an inverted index — a map from every word to the logs containing it — so text queries with filters return in milliseconds instead of scanning data."
2. **Why we picked it:** the query requirement is ranked free-text plus field filters (service, level, time) over ~77 TB hot in seconds — only an inverted-index engine does that; a relational scan can't.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **PostgreSQL/`LIKE`** — would full-scan every row per query with no ranking; unusable at this size.
   - **ClickHouse** — fantastic for analytics aggregations and cheaper to store, but weaker at ad-hoc full-text/fuzzy search, which is the debugging use case. (Strong alternative — I'd keep it in mind if queries skew analytical.)
   - **Grep on S3 files** — no index, minutes per query.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** ES is RAM/disk-hungry and pricey, so we keep only 7 days hot and tier the rest to S3; search is seconds-fresh, not instant.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "For debugging search I use Elasticsearch because developers want free-text over the last week in seconds, and only an inverted index does that. It's expensive, so I keep just 7 days hot and roll indices by day. I considered ClickHouse — cheaper, great for aggregations — but it's weaker on ad-hoc text search, which is what debugging actually needs."

**🗄️ S3 (object storage) — used for: LogEvent (30-day cold archive)**
1. **What it is (plain words):** "S3 is cheap, durable bulk file storage billed per gigabyte — you put compressed files in and they're safe basically forever, but you can't search them without scanning."
2. **Why we picked it:** we need a 30-day archive at the lowest $/TB and a rebuild source for the index; S3 is ~10x cheaper than keeping it in ES.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **Keep 30 days in ES** — ~10x the cost for data queried rarely; wasteful.
   - **HDFS** — self-managed, more ops burden than managed object storage.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** cold queries are slow (scan/restore, seconds-to-minutes); acceptable for rare old-data lookups.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Anything older than a week goes to S3 as compressed files — it's dirt cheap and durable. The cost is that searching cold data is slow, but people almost never debug week-old logs interactively, so that's a fine trade."

**🗄️ PostgreSQL — used for: AlertRule, AlertEvent record, ServiceSource**
1. **What it is (plain words):** "Postgres is a relational database with rows, columns, and transactions — it guarantees a multi-step change either fully happens or fully rolls back."
2. **Why we picked it:** rules and service metadata are low-volume, relational, and need transactional CRUD and joins — classic relational fit.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **DynamoDB** — fine as KV, but rule management benefits from relational queries/joins and there's no scale pressure here.
   - **Storing rules only in Flink memory** — lost on restart, no CRUD/audit.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** none material — it's a small, well-understood store far from the hot path.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Rules and metadata go in plain Postgres — it's a few thousand rows, needs transactions and a UI to manage, and it's nowhere near the firehose, so there's zero scale pressure."

**🗄️ Redis — used for: AlertEvent dedup**
1. **What it is (plain words):** "Redis is an in-memory key-value store with atomic operations and per-key expiry — extremely fast for 'set this key if it doesn't exist, and forget it in 5 minutes.'"
2. **Why we picked it:** dedup needs a sub-millisecond atomic check-and-set on (rule, window) shared across all Flink tasks, with auto-expiry = cooldown.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **Postgres unique constraint** — too slow/hot for per-fire checks at scale.
   - **Flink local state** — not shared across parallel tasks, so duplicates leak.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** Redis is volatile — on failure we fail *open* (page anyway), accepting rare duplicates over missed alerts.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Dedup is a Redis set-if-absent on the rule plus the time window, with the cooldown as the expiry. It's shared and atomic, so even with many parallel alert tasks you get paged once. If Redis is down I fail open — I'd rather double-page than miss a real fire."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Append log (ack) | Kafka | Strong (durable, replicated) | Zero-loss promise — must persist before ack |
| Search recent logs | Elasticsearch | Eventual (secs behind) | Debugging tolerates seconds of lag |
| Search old logs | S3 | Eventual + slow | Rare; cost beats freshness |
| Create/edit rule | Postgres | Strong (transactional) | Rules are control plane; must be correct |
| Alert dedup | Redis | Eventual (fail-open) | Better to double-page than miss |
| Record AlertEvent | Postgres | Strong | Audit trail of what fired |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The data lives in five places, each chosen for its job. Kafka is the durable firehose buffer — strong durability so we never lose an acked log. Elasticsearch holds 7 days hot for fast text search, eventual freshness is fine. S3 is the cheap 30-day cold tier. Postgres holds the rules and metadata — small, relational, transactional, far off the hot path. And Redis does alert dedup with atomic set-and-expire. The one 'I considered but rejected' I'd flag: ClickHouse instead of Elasticsearch — cheaper and great for aggregations, but weaker at the ad-hoc full-text search that debugging actually needs, so ES wins for the query use case."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to "1M/s write vs 1/s read" — every hard call traces to *decouple the firehose, push cost down the tiers, never block the sender.*

### How do we ingest 1M+ logs/s without losing data or blocking senders?

**#### Bad: synchronous write to a database**
🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** "Each service writes its log straight into a shared database and waits for an OK."
**Approach:** service → `INSERT` into Postgres/ES → wait for ack.
**Why people try this:** it's the obvious, simplest thing and it works in a demo.
⚠️ **What breaks:** no single DB takes 1M inserts/s — write amplification, index updates, and lock contention cap a tuned Postgres around ~10–50K/s. At 1M/s the DB is 20x over, connection pools exhaust, senders block on the write and their *own* latency spikes. During a log storm (exactly when you need logs) it's first to die.
🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** the moment write rate exceeds a single DB's ceiling — i.e. immediately — you need a buffer that decouples accept from store.

**#### Good: buffer behind a durable log (Kafka) + stateless collectors**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad coupled the sender to the slow store; we split them — accept fast into a durable buffer, process slowly downstream.
🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** "Senders drop logs into a durable mailbox that instantly says 'got it,' and separate workers process the mailbox at their own pace."
**Approach:** stateless collectors authenticate + stamp + append a batch to **Kafka** (a partitioned, replicated, on-disk log), ack 202 on durable append. Consumers (alert/index) read independently. Partition by `service_id`; ~150 partitions give parallelism.
**Why reasonable:** decoupling write from read is the canonical firehose pattern.
⚠️ **What breaks:** a few hot services (a noisy monolith) dominate one partition → that partition's consumer falls behind while others idle — *partition skew*. Also a single global topic can hot-spot, and cross-AZ replication adds latency.
🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** uneven service volume creating hot partitions, plus the need to absorb 2x storms without dropping low-value logs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Services] -->|"append batch"| K[[Kafka ~150 partitions x3]]
    K -->|"consume"| C1[Alert consumer]
    K -->|"consume"| C2[Index consumer]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    class S,C1,C2 svc
    class K queue
```

**#### Great: keyed partitioning + back-pressure + severity-aware load shedding**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good let a few loud services skew partitions and had no plan for a storm beyond capacity; we add smart keying and graceful shedding.
🗣️ **What this is in plain words:** "We spread load so no one service can swamp a single lane, and if the firehose ever truly exceeds capacity, we drop the *cheap* logs first and never the errors."
**Approach:**
- **Composite partition key** `hash(service_id + bucket)` so a single huge service spreads across several partitions instead of pinning one (a *bucketing* / sub-keying trick).
- **Back-pressure:** collectors ack only after Kafka append; if Kafka is saturated, return 429 and clients exponential-backoff + buffer locally — durable on both ends.
- **Severity-aware shedding:** under sustained overload, a config flag tells collectors/clients to sample DEBUG/INFO (e.g. keep 1 in 10) but **never** drop WARN/ERROR. Errors are the point.
- **Idempotent producer** (Kafka producer dedup via sequence numbers) so a retried batch isn't double-written.
**Why this seemed reasonable / why it's the answer:** it keeps the system *up and useful* precisely during the storms that motivate it.
🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** 2M/s peak ÷ ~50K/s per collector ≈ **40 collectors**; 1 GB/s ÷ ~10 MB/s per partition ≈ **~100–150 partitions**. One hot service at 200K/s would pin 1 partition (10 MB/s ceiling) → bucket into ~20 sub-keys to stay under per-partition limits.
✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Collector dies | LB pulls it; batch retried | Stateless, idempotent producer dedups |
| Kafka broker dies | Replica promoted | RF=3, in-flight batch retried |
| Kafka cluster saturated | Collectors return 429 | Client backoff + local buffer; shed DEBUG/INFO |
| Duplicate batch (retry) | Same events twice | Idempotent producer + doc id = partition+offset downstream |
| Hot service skews partition | One consumer lags | Composite key buckets the service across partitions |
| Total Kafka outage | No ingest | Clients buffer to local disk, replay on recovery; alerting blind — page on consumer-lag SLO |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Ingest is solved by a durable buffer. Senders append batches to Kafka — a replicated on-disk log — and we ack only once it's safely persisted, which is our zero-loss line. The two subtleties that make it 'great': first, I partition on service *plus a bucket* so one chatty service can't pin a single lane and starve its consumer; second, I plan for the storm — if we ever exceed capacity, collectors push back with 429s and clients back off, and we shed DEBUG and INFO logs while never touching errors. The math is roughly 40 stateless collectors and ~150 partitions for 2 million a second."
🧠 **If they ask "what if Kafka itself is down?":** "Clients buffer to local disk and replay on recovery, so we still don't lose acked data — but alerting goes blind, so I put a hard SLO alarm on consumer lag and Kafka availability; that outage is the one I page *myself* on."

### How do we evaluate thousands of alert rules at 1M/s with p99 < 1s?

**#### Bad: periodic SQL query per rule**
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Every few seconds, run a `COUNT(*)` query per rule against the log store."
**Approach:** cron every N seconds: `SELECT count(*) ... WHERE service=X AND level=ERROR AND ts > now()-60s`.
**Why people try this:** it reuses the search store; feels natural.
⚠️ **What breaks:** at 1M/s the table is enormous; each count scans a huge window, thousands of rules = thousands of heavy scans hammering the store, and you get *minutes* of latency, not sub-second. It also competes with debugging queries.
🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** the sub-second SLO — querying stored data can't be continuous or fast enough.

**#### Good: streaming windowed counters (Flink keyed state)**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Bad re-scanned stored data repeatedly; we instead compute incrementally as events pass, holding counts in memory.
🗣️ **Plain words:** "We keep a live tally per rule in memory and bump it as each log flies by, so checking a threshold is reading a number, not searching a database."
**Approach:** Flink consumes Kafka, keys events by (service, rule-dimension), maintains **sliding-window counters** in memory (a window = a moving time bucket like last 60s), emits when a counter crosses the threshold. Pattern rules apply a compiled regex per event. Per-event work is O(rules-matching-that-key), effectively O(1).
**Why reasonable:** incremental streaming aggregation is the standard sub-second answer.
⚠️ **What breaks:** state lives in memory — a crash loses windows; and a rule spike pages repeatedly without dedup; clock skew on event_time corrupts windows.
🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** need crash-safe state, dedup, and skew handling for correctness.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka]] -->|"events"| FL[Flink keyed windows]
    FL -->|"counter > threshold"| EM[Emit candidate]
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class FL,EM svc
```

**#### Great: checkpointed state + event-time windows + Redis dedup + fail-open**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good was fast but fragile — no crash recovery, no dedup, naive on time; we harden all three.
🗣️ **Plain words:** "We make the live tallies crash-proof, base windows on a trustworthy clock, and guarantee one page per incident, not five hundred."
**Approach:**
- **Checkpointing:** Flink snapshots state + Kafka offsets to durable storage every few seconds; on crash it restores and replays, so no window is lost (exactly-once *state*).
- **Event-time windows with watermarks** keyed on server `received_at` (a *watermark* tells Flink "no earlier events are coming, close the window"), so out-of-order/late logs don't corrupt counts.
- **Redis dedup:** on a candidate fire, `SET rule_id:window NX EX cooldown`; only the first winner pages. Fail-open if Redis is down.
- **Notifier decoupling:** delivery is async with retries so pager I/O never stalls the stream.
**Why it's the answer:** it's the only version that's simultaneously sub-second, crash-safe, and humane to the on-call.
🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** counter update is ~O(1) per event; 1M events/s × a few rule-dimensions each ≈ a few M state ops/s, well within a sharded Flink cluster's reach across ~150 parallel tasks. End-to-end budget: Kafka append ~50ms + Flink window/eval ~100–300ms + Redis ~1ms + notify enqueue ~10ms ≈ **well under 1s p99**.
✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Flink task crashes | Window state at risk | Restore from checkpoint + replay offset |
| Redis down | Dedup unavailable | Fail open: page anyway (rare dupes) |
| Pager API down | Alert undelivered | Notifier retries + secondary channel |
| Late/out-of-order logs | Window miscount | Event-time + watermark grace period |
| Rule storm (sustained) | Repeated fires | Dedup key + cooldown TTL |
| Duplicate event from retry | Double count | Idempotent producer upstream + dedup at fire |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Alerting is a Flink job holding live windowed counters in memory, so a threshold check is reading a number, not querying a database — that's how we get sub-second. To make it correct, I checkpoint Flink's state and Kafka offsets every few seconds so a crash loses no window; I window on server receive-time with watermarks so late logs don't corrupt counts; and I dedup each fire through a Redis set-with-expiry so a sustained spike pages you once. Delivery is async so a slow pager can't back up the stream. Budget-wise, append plus window-eval plus dedup plus enqueue lands comfortably under a second."
🧠 **If they ask "exactly-once alerting?":** "I give exactly-once *state* via checkpointing, but delivery is at-least-once deduped to once-per-window — true end-to-end exactly-once across an external pager is impractical, and double-paging once in a blue moon beats missing a real alert."

### How do we keep 7 days searchable without the cost exploding?

**#### Good: everything in Elasticsearch**
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Index every log fully so any search is instant."
**Approach:** index all fields + full body in ES for the full retention.
⚠️ **What breaks:** full-text indexing 88 TB/day with replication is enormous RAM/SSD — index overhead can exceed the raw data; cost is multiples of the product being monitored. Most logs are never searched.
🔁 **What forces the upgrade:** cost — indexing the long tail nobody reads.

**#### Great: hot/cold tiering + selective indexing + index rollover**
↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** Good indexed everything forever; we index *less, for less time*, and tier the rest.
🗣️ **Plain words:** "Keep the last week fully searchable, index only the fields people actually filter on, and shove everything else into cheap cold storage."
**Approach:**
- **7 days hot in ES**, 30 days cold in **S3** (compressed); delete after.
- **Selective indexing:** index `service, level, trace_id, event_time` as keyword/numeric (cheap) and the `msg` as analyzed text; keep big `attrs`/stack traces as a stored-but-not-fully-analyzed blob — index what people filter/search on, store the rest.
- **Time-based rollover + ILM** (index lifecycle management): one index per day; aging out = delete an index (O(1)); warm tier on cheaper disks before cold.
- **Force-merge + best-compression** on rolled (read-only) indices to shrink footprint.
🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** 11 TB/day compressed × 7 = ~77 TB hot vs ~330 TB for 30 days — keeping 30 days hot would roughly **4x** the ES fleet for data queried <5% as often. Tiering cuts hot cost ~75%.
✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| ES node fails | Shard unavailable | Replica shard serves; reindex from Kafka/S3 |
| Indexer lags | Search stale | Kafka holds backlog; catch up, no loss |
| Hot disk fills | Index rejects writes | ILM rollover + watermark-based eviction to warm |
| Cold query needed | Slow | Async restore/scan from S3, set expectations |

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Search cost is controlled by tiering. I keep 7 days hot in Elasticsearch and index only the fields people actually filter on — service, level, trace id, and the message text — while bulky stack traces are stored but not fully indexed. I roll a fresh index every day, so dropping old data is just deleting an index, and I force-merge old indices to shrink them. Everything past a week lives compressed in S3. Keeping 30 days hot would roughly quadruple the search fleet for data almost nobody queries, so tiering is the single biggest cost lever."
🧠 **If they ask "what if someone needs 30-day-old logs fast?":** "They can, but it's an async export from S3 measured in minutes — I optimize the hot path for the 95% case and make the rare deep lookup possible, not instant."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean)**
- **Ingest path: four nines (99.99%)** — "down less than ~52 minutes per year total." Achieved by stateless collectors behind a load balancer across **multi-AZ** (*multi-AZ* = several independent data-centre buildings in one region, so if one loses power the others keep serving), and Kafka with replication factor 3 across AZs.
- **Alert path: 99.9%** ("~8.7 hours/year") — acceptable because alerting tolerates brief blips and we alarm on its own health; Flink with checkpointing + standby recovers in seconds.
- **Query path: 99.9%** — debugging can survive a short outage; ES with replica shards.
The asymmetry is deliberate: **ingest durability is sacred** (lose nothing acked); alerting/search availability is important but not life-or-death because Kafka holds the data for replay.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the user sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load balancer | No ingest entry | Multi-AZ LB + DNS failover; senders retry | Auto-failover to standby LB |
| Ingest collector | Less ingest capacity | Others absorb; clients buffer/backoff — "logs delayed seconds" | Autoscale replaces node |
| Kafka | Ingest + all consumers blind | Clients buffer locally; **alerting stops** — page on-call directly | RF=3 failover; replay backlog |
| Flink (alerting) | No new alerts | "Alerts paused" banner; replay from checkpoint on recovery | Standby task restores checkpoint |
| Redis (dedup) | No dedup | Fail open: possible duplicate pages, never missed | Restart; keys re-populate (TTL) |
| Elasticsearch | Search degraded | "Search temporarily slow/limited to recent" | Replica shards; reindex from Kafka/S3 |
| S3 (cold) | No old-data search | Recent (hot) search unaffected | S3 is 11-nines; transient retry |
| Notifier/PagerDuty | Alerts not delivered | Secondary channel (Slack/SMS) | Retry queue + failover channel |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**
**RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we're OK losing if it all crashes right now. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is back.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acked logs (in Kafka) | **≈0** | < 1 min | RF=3 sync replication before ack; broker failover |
| Alert rules (Postgres) | ≈0 | < 5 min | Synchronous replica + PITR backups |
| Alert windows (Flink state) | seconds | < 30s | Checkpoint + replay from Kafka offset |
| Hot search index (ES) | minutes (rebuildable) | < 30 min | Reindex from Kafka/S3 — index is derived, not source of truth |
| Cold archive (S3) | ≈0 | n/a | 11-nines durability |

The key insight: **only Kafka and Postgres are sources of truth needing RPO≈0; ES is derived and fully rebuildable**, which lets us run it cheaper (fewer replicas) without risking data.

**9D — Cost breakdown**
Top 3 drivers, plain terms:
1. **Elasticsearch hot tier (likely #1)** — RAM + SSD to index and serve 77 TB hot with replicas; indexing is CPU-heavy. *Biggest lever:* selective indexing + 7-day cap + force-merge (cutting hot retention or indexed fields cuts this most).
2. **Kafka storage + cross-AZ traffic** — holding the firehose with RF=3 and replicating across AZs; cross-AZ data transfer is a real line item at ~1 GB/s.
3. **S3 storage + retrieval** — cheap per GB but 330 TB/month adds up; lifecycle to Glacier for >7-day-old cold data trims it.
Rough order of magnitude: **mid-to-high six figures/month** at this scale, dominated by ES. The single biggest optimization: **drop hot retention/indexed-field count** — most cost is indexing logs nobody searches.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The failure story centers on one rule: never lose an acked log. Kafka with three-way replication is the source of truth, so even if Flink, Elasticsearch, or Redis fall over, the data's safe and those derived systems just replay and rebuild — a user might see 'alerts paused' or 'search limited to recent' for a few minutes, never lost data. The thing I page myself on is Kafka health and consumer lag, because if Kafka's down we're blind. On cost, Elasticsearch dominates because indexing is expensive, so the biggest lever is keeping only a week hot and indexing only the fields people filter on."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: Kafka durable buffer vs writing straight to the datastore**
1. **What we chose and why:** a durable log buffer in front of everything — it's the only way to accept 1M/s, guarantee zero acked-loss, and fan out to 3 consumers.
2. **What we gave up:** operational complexity (running/balancing Kafka) and a few tens of ms of added latency before data is queryable.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We put a big durable mailbox in front so the firehose can never overwhelm or be lost, instead of everyone writing directly to one fragile box."
4. **When this reverses:** if volume dropped to, say, <50K/s, a single durable DB (or managed stream like Kinesis) could absorb it directly and we'd drop the Kafka ops burden.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "At a million a second the buffer isn't optional — but if this were a small internal tool I'd skip Kafka and write straight to the store."

**🔀 Decision: at-least-once + dedup alerting vs exactly-once**
1. **What we chose and why:** at-least-once evaluation deduped to once-per-window via Redis — simple, fast, sub-second.
2. **What we gave up:** a guarantee of *never* double-paging; under a Redis failure we may page twice.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We guarantee you're never *missed*, and almost never double-notified — we chose 'better twice than never.'"
4. **When this reverses:** if an alert triggered an *automated, non-idempotent action* (e.g. auto-scaling spend or auto-remediation), I'd invest in transactional exactly-once because a duplicate would have real cost.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Alerts are at-least-once with dedup. For paging a human that's perfect — missing an alert is the unforgivable failure, a rare duplicate is just mildly annoying."

**🔀 Decision: 7-day hot ES + cold S3 vs all-hot search**
1. **What we chose and why:** tiered storage — only a week indexed hot — because indexing dominates cost and old logs are rarely searched.
2. **What we gave up:** instant search over older data; cold queries are slow (minutes).
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We keep the last week instantly searchable and everything older in a cheap basement you can still dig through, just slower."
4. **When this reverses:** a compliance/forensics requirement to search 90 days interactively would force more hot tier (or a cheaper engine like ClickHouse for the warm tier).
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Seven days hot, thirty days cold in S3. If compliance demanded fast 90-day search I'd rethink — maybe ClickHouse for a cheaper warm tier — but for debugging, a week covers it."

**🔀 Decision: severity-aware load shedding vs always accept everything**
1. **What we chose and why:** under extreme overload, sample DEBUG/INFO but never errors — keeps the system up during storms when it matters most.
2. **What we gave up:** completeness of low-value logs during the worst moments.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "If the firehose ever truly exceeds our capacity, we quietly drop the boring logs first so the important error logs always get through."
4. **When this reverses:** if a customer needs *every* log for audit/legal even under overload, we'd over-provision to never shed and accept the cost.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I'd rather shed DEBUG logs than drop errors or fall over. If audit rules forbid dropping anything, I'd pay to over-provision instead."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least dogmatic about: the Kafka buffer is non-negotiable at this scale but I'd drop it for a small system; alerting is at-least-once with dedup, which is right for paging humans but I'd upgrade to exactly-once if an alert triggered automated spend; and the 7-day hot window is a cost call I'd revisit the instant compliance needed longer interactive retention. Each one traces straight back to the opening trade: spend on the write path, keep the read path cheap, and never lose an acked log."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "At a million logs a second, what's the actual binding bottleneck, and how do you scale past it?"**
**The mechanism:** the binding constraint is per-partition throughput in Kafka (~10 MB/s/partition) and per-consumer keyed-state ops in Flink. We scale by adding partitions (~150 for 1 GB/s) and matching Flink parallelism; collectors are stateless so they scale freely. The ceiling after that is broker disk I/O and cross-AZ bandwidth.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "The firehose is split into ~150 lanes; if we need more, we add lanes and matching workers. Nothing is a single chokepoint because the collectors are clones."
**💬 One-liner:** "It's horizontally partitioned end to end — more volume just means more partitions and more stateless workers."

**❓ "What happens when Kafka goes down entirely?"**
**The mechanism:** RF=3 across AZs means a single broker or AZ loss triggers replica promotion with no data loss. A total cluster outage is the doomsday case: clients buffer logs to local disk and replay on recovery, so acked data survives, but alerting and indexing go blind. We put a hard SLO alarm on Kafka availability and consumer lag and page on-call directly.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "One broker dying is a non-event — a copy takes over. The whole cluster dying means senders hold logs locally and replay later, but we're temporarily blind, so that's the thing we watch hardest."
**💬 One-liner:** "Three-way replication makes broker loss a non-event; total loss is survivable via client buffering but it's the alarm I care most about."

**❓ "A deploy causes every service to error at once — a log storm. Does the system survive?"**
**The mechanism:** we size for 2x peak (~40 collectors, ~150 partitions). Beyond that, back-pressure (429 + client backoff) plus severity-aware shedding (sample DEBUG/INFO, never errors) keeps us up. Kafka absorbs the burst on disk and consumers catch up after.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Storms are exactly what we're built for — we buffer the spike, push back gently, and if it's truly extreme we drop boring logs but never the errors."
**💬 One-liner:** "We provision for 2x, buffer the rest in Kafka, and shed DEBUG before we ever touch an error log."

**❓ "Can the same alert fire 500 times during a sustained spike?"**
**The mechanism:** no — each fire does an atomic Redis `SET rule_id:window NX EX cooldown`; only the first wins within the cooldown window, the rest are suppressed. If Redis is down we fail open (may double-fire, never silent).
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We remember we already paged you for this, and stay quiet until the cooldown passes."
**💬 One-liner:** "Dedup is a Redis set-with-expiry on rule-plus-window — one page per incident, fail-open if Redis dies."

**❓ "Can you lose a log? Where exactly is the durability boundary?"**
**The mechanism:** we ack 202 only *after* Kafka confirms a replicated append (RF=3). Before that, the client retries; after that, it's durable. Downstream ES/S3 are derived and rebuildable from Kafka, so losing an ES node loses no data.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We only say 'got it' once the log is safely copied three times. Everything after that can be rebuilt from Kafka."
**💬 One-liner:** "Zero loss on acked logs — the ack happens only after a 3x-replicated Kafka write."

**❓ "Can the same log be indexed or counted twice on a retry?"**
**The mechanism:** the Kafka idempotent producer dedups retried batches by sequence number; downstream, ES doc id = `partition+offset`, so re-indexing the same event is an overwrite, not a duplicate. Alert counts can briefly over-count on replay but windows close and dedup bounds user impact.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Retries can't create duplicate stored logs because each log has a fixed address; writing it twice just overwrites the same slot."
**💬 One-liner:** "Idempotent producer plus offset-based doc ids make indexing exactly-once in effect."

**❓ "How do you stop a service from forging another service's identity or spoofing timestamps?"**
**The mechanism:** senders authenticate with a per-service API key or mTLS; the collector derives the *trusted* `service_id` from the credential, not the payload, and stamps a server `received_at`. Client `event_time` is kept but never trusted for windowing or routing.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We trust who you proved you are at the door, not what you wrote on the envelope — and we stamp our own clock."
**💬 One-liner:** "Identity and timestamp come from the authenticated connection and our server clock, never the request body."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut the bill?"**
**The mechanism:** Elasticsearch hot tier dominates — indexing 77 TB hot with replicas is RAM/SSD/CPU heavy. Biggest lever: cut hot retention or indexed-field count, force-merge old indices, and tier aggressively to S3/Glacier. Cross-AZ Kafka traffic is #2.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Search indexing is the big bill because most logs are never searched — so we index less, for less time, and dump the rest in cheap storage."
**💬 One-liner:** "ES is the cost; the fix is selective indexing and a short hot window — don't pay to index logs nobody reads."

**❓ "How would you add anomaly detection (spikes vs a learned baseline) later?"**
**The mechanism:** it's another Flink consumer on the same Kafka stream — maintain rolling baselines per service (EWMA/seasonal) in state and fire when current rate deviates by N sigma. The fan-out architecture means new consumers are additive, no ingest changes.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We just add another reader on the same firehose that learns each service's normal and flags weird — nothing upstream changes."
**💬 One-liner:** "New analytics = a new Kafka consumer; the stream fan-out makes features additive."

**❓ "How do you handle multi-region — services on two continents?"**
**The mechanism:** regional ingest + regional Kafka (data stays local, low latency, sovereignty), with alerting evaluated per-region; a global rule/metadata layer replicates rules. For global queries, federate the search across regional ES clusters or aggregate to a central warehouse asynchronously.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "Each region ingests and alerts on its own logs locally, and we stitch together a global view for search separately."
**💬 One-liner:** "Ingest and alert locally per region; federate or async-aggregate for the global picture."

**❓ "Why Flink and not just Kafka Streams or a Lambda function per event?"**
**The mechanism:** Flink gives windowed keyed state, event-time/watermarks, and checkpointed exactly-once state at this scale out of the box; Kafka Streams can do much of it but heavy stateful scaling and event-time handling are more mature in Flink. Per-event Lambda has no shared windowed state and would be cost-prohibitive at 1M/s.
**🗣️ In plain words:** "We need live, crash-proof running tallies over time windows — that's exactly what Flink is built for; functions-per-event can't hold that shared state."
**💬 One-liner:** "Flink owns windowed, checkpointed state at scale — the core of sub-second alerting."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a real-time error-log monitoring system that swallows over a million logs a second from hundreds of services and does three things with them: alerts in under a second, lets developers search for debugging, and never loses an acked log. The design splits into two halves around one durable Kafka log. The read half is search: an indexer feeds Elasticsearch with a 7-day hot inverted index for fast free-text queries, and dumps everything older to cheap S3 — we tier hard because indexing, not storage, is the cost. The write/real-time half is alerting: a Flink job reads the same stream, keeps windowed counters in memory so threshold checks are sub-second, checkpoints its state so a crash loses nothing, and dedups each fire through a Redis set-with-expiry so a spike pages you once, not five hundred times. The two halves connect through that single Kafka stream — every consumer reads it independently and scales on its own. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: it's a hundred-thousand-to-one write-to-read system, so spend everything on a durable, sharded firehose and keep the read path cheap — and never, ever lose an acked log."
